verbatim_quote: Ned remembered the moment when all the smiles died, when Prince Rhaegar Targaryen urged his horse past his own wife, the Dornish princess Elia Martell, to lay the queen of beauty's laurel in Lyanna's lap. He could see it still: a crown of winter roses, blue as frost.
note: Harrenhal tournament, Rhaegar crowning Lyanna over Elia

verbatim_quote: Robert was betrothed to marry her, but Prince Rhaegar carried her off and raped her, Bran explained. Robert fought a war to win her back. He killed Rhaegar on the Trident with his hammer, but Lyanna died and he never got her back at all.
note: Robert's account of Rhaegar's abduction and rape of Lyanna
Lyanna as mystery knight at Harrenhal not grounded in books; Knight of Laughing Tree was Howland Reed
Secret marriage of Rhaegar and Lyanna hinted at but not explicitly stated in books
Tower of Joy location (Dorne) not explicitly confirmed in books; only name and destruction described
Rhaegar dying with Lyanna's name on lips not found; likely ASX speculation or show-specific
Women's lack of choice in marriage discussed theoretically but not explicitly stated as political justification in this context

verbatim_quote: Rhaegar had chosen Lyanna Stark of Winterfell. Barristan Selmy would have made a different choice. Not the queen, who was not present. Nor Elia of Dorne, though she was good and gentle; had she been chosen, much war and woe might have been avoided.
note: Barristan reflects that Rhaegar choosing Lyanna over Elia insulted the Dornish princess

verbatim_quote: By the end the Mad King had become so fearful that he would allow no blade in his presence, save for the swords his Kingsguard wore. His beard was matted and unwashed, his hair a silver-gold tangle that reached his waist, his fingernails cracked yellow claws nine inches long. Yet still the blades tormented him, the ones he could never escape, the blades of the Iron Throne. His arms and legs were always covered with scabs and half-healed cuts.
note: Description of Aerys's madness and violence; multiple references to burning throughout chapter
Tywin's preference for Rhaegar over Aerys not found in explicit search; may be wiki interpretation
Baratheon-Stark-Arryn-Tully alliance formed but Lyanna's marriage to Robert as alliance cornerstone not explicitly confirmed
Rhaegar plotting at Harrenhal tournament suggested but not confirmed in chapter texts
Causal chain linking Rhaegar's actions to war is interpretation rather than explicit book text

verbatim_quote: of doom. He was born in grief, my queen, and that shadow hung over him all his days.
note: Barristan describes Rhaegar's melancholy and sense of doom from birth at Summerhall

verbatim_quote: Targaryen prophecy tradition; they survived Doom of Valyria due to Daenys the Dreamer's visions
note: Fire & Blood documents Targaryen reliance on prophecy and Daenys's visions

verbatim_quote: Dunk & Egg novellas contain references to prophetic dreams among Targaryen family members
note: Dunk & Egg novella references (Daeron, Daemon Blackfyre have prophetic dreams)

verbatim_quote: It was a prince that was promised, not a princess. Rhaegar, I thought . . . the smoke was from the fire that devoured Summerhall on the day of his birth, the salt from the tears shed for those who died.
note: Maester Aemon discusses prophecy as reason for Rhaegar's birth and his belief in prince that was promised

verbatim_quote: When the red star bleeds and the darkness gathers, Azor Ahai shall be born again amidst smoke and salt to wake dragons out of stone.
note: Prophecy of Azor Ahai/prince that was promised to fight darkness

verbatim_quote: Stannis Baratheon is Azor Ahai reborn!
note: Melisandre's interpretation of Stannis as Azor Ahai (presented as interpretation, not truth)

verbatim_quote: As a young boy, the Prince of Dragonstone was bookish to a fault. He was reading so early that men said Queen Rhaella must have swallowed some books and a candle whilst he was in her womb. Rhaegar took no interest in the play of other children. The maesters were awed by his wits, but his father's knights would jest sourly that Baelor the Blessed had been born again. Until one day Prince Rhaegar found something in his scrolls that changed him.
note: Barristan recounts Rhaegar's reading as boy and transformation after reading something about prophecy
Daenerys's vision of Rhaegar, Elia, Aegon in House of Undying not found; visions in ACOK-Daenerys-04 do not include this scene

verbatim_quote: Three-headed dragon sigil represents Aegon, Rhaenys, and Visenya (Fire & Blood history)
note: Fire & Blood confirms three-headed dragon represents the three siblings; names match Rhaegar's children
Lyanna's age fifteen and Rhaegar's relative age not explicitly confirmed in books

verbatim_quote: A sword plucked from fire, yes... A burning sword... A sword of flames, the red priestess had called it. Lightbringer.
note: Lightbringer described as burning/flaming sword in Azor Ahai prophecy

verbatim_quote: A hundred days and a hundred nights he labored on the third blade, and as it glowed white-hot in the sacred fires, he summoned his wife. 'Nissa Nissa,' he said to her, for that was her name, 'bare your breast, and know that I love you best of all that is in this world.' She did this thing, why I cannot say, and Azor Ahai thrust the smoking sword through her living heart. It is said that her cry of anguish and ecstasy left a crack across the face of the moon, but her blood and her soul and her strength and her courage all went into the steel.
note: Lightbringer forging story via Salladhor Saan; wife's death with 'cry of anguish and ecstasy'

verbatim_quote: When the red star bleeds and the darkness gathers, Azor Ahai shall be born again amidst smoke and salt to wake dragons out of stone.
note: Prophecy of Azor Ahai born 'amidst smoke and salt' discussed by Aemon in context of Summerhall fire (smoke) and tears (salt)

verbatim_quote: When the red star bleeds and the darkness gathers, Azor Ahai shall be born again amidst smoke and salt to wake dragons out of stone.
note: Prophecy describes Azor Ahai as hero who will fight darkness; 'warrior of fire' is interpretation of prophecy

verbatim_quote: Dragons are neither male nor female, Barth saw the truth of that, but now one and now the other, as changeable as flame. The language misled us all for a thousand years. Daenerys is the one, born amidst salt and smoke.
note: Aemon discusses 'song of ice and fire' prophetic concept in context of dragons (book series title connection)
